chapters.title: Ukraine's rising technology sector
chapters.text:
The idea to showcase Ukraine's digital achievements came from Andrei Kirilenko. After reviewing the country's economic performance across twenty-five years, he spotted a striking contrast: uneven financial results alongside a fast-growing information technology sector that kept expanding despite political and economic headwinds. Researching that puzzle produced the insights collected here.

I certainly believe that people who have contributed so much to global civilization in the digital realm can rise to the challenges, take bold actions, and find ways to prosper and live well. ~ Andrei Kirilenko, PhD

Ukraine's IT story diverges from typical national tech narratives because the twentieth and twenty-first centuries brought upheaval rather than steady institutional support. Historians often divide Ukrainian computing into three generations:

• Post-war IT in the Ukrainian SSR
• IT during Perestroika
• Contemporary IT in independent Ukraine

Each cohort carried distinct methods and mindsets. The gaps between them feel less like smooth evolution and more like turbulent leaps — yet the present industry remains genuinely impressive by global standards.

Each generation's history and mentality shape every field, from art to engineering.

While the two previous generations faced restrictions of socialist traditions and the heavy weight of Soviet atrocities inhibiting their progress, the contemporary cohort is free of those limitations. Modern Ukrainian IT specialists are flexible, inventive, and up-to-date with the latest advancements in the field.

chapters.title: Pioneers of Ukrainian computing
chapters.text:
Ukraine's digital heritage begins with remarkable inventors who influenced machines worldwide. A few representative figures illustrate the depth of that lineage.

Sergey Lebedev, born in 1902, was an accomplished electrical engineering researcher. In 1944 the Ukrainian Academy of Sciences invited him to direct the Institute of Energy Sciences in Kyiv, where his career accelerated. By 1948 he was building a digital computer; in 1950 he completed the MESM (Small Electronic Computing Machine), among the earliest working prototypes on the planet. He later upgraded the design into the BESM (Big Electronic Computing Machine), launched in 1953. BESM became Europe's fastest mass-produced computer and competed seriously with IBM offerings of the era.

Kateryna Yushchenko followed a harder road. Stalinist authorities persecuted her father, delaying her progress, yet she joined the Institute of Mathematics at the Ukrainian Academy of Sciences, worked in probability theory, and in 1952 entered Lebedev's MESM team. She created an address programming language that accelerated coding dramatically. It predated many famous alternatives and powered Soviet computers for two decades.

Borys Malynovskyi, Lebedev's student, led hardware design for the Dnieper — the first mass-produced computer compatible with industrial automated control systems. Production approval arrived in 1952, ahead of comparable machines from leading American firms.

Both personal ambition and social climate determine success.

Viktor Glushkov designed the OGAS (All State Automated System for the Gathering and Processing of Information), an ambitious network meant to decentralize economic data and cut bureaucracy. Funding never arrived; the Finance Ministry blocked a system that threatened corrupt intermediaries.

Viktor Glushkov is another great mind of this period. He is most known for creating the revolutionary All State Automated System for the Gathering and Processing of Information (OGAS). The OGAS was an attempt to develop a remote-access computer network that would help run the economy of the whole country by decentralizing information and eradicating bureaucracy. Unfortunately, the government never put Glushkov's system to work — the Ministry of Finance blocked its funding because the OGAS had the potential to become a formidable defense against corruption.

Did you know? BESM-4 (the fourth model of Lebedev's computer) was used to create the first-ever computer animation.

chapters.title: Breaking free after independence
chapters.text:
When Ukraine regained independence in 1991, the IT sector reshaped itself alongside society. Soviet habits faded slowly while new companies experimented with market realities.

The 1990s brought firms such as Kvazar-Micro, central to national infrastructure, and ELEKS, a family-founded business now serving more than two hundred international clients. SoftServe illustrates the scale of change: launched in Lviv in 1993 with fewer than ten staff around a single desk, it now employs over eight thousand people.

From the 2000s onward, Ukrainian technology reached global competitiveness. Indicators moved sharply:

• Industry share of GDP climbed from 0.6% to 3.3% between 2011 and 2015 (roughly $1.1 billion to $2.7 billion).
• IT employment rose from 42,400 to 91,700 in the same window.

Today more than four thousand established firms operate domestically, with newcomers appearing constantly. Names like GlobalLogic, Netcracker, EPAM, Intellias, Genesis, EVO, and Terrasoft circulate widely abroad.

A solid foundation for the Ukrainian IT market is the many local IT Clusters working to strengthen the professional community, provide education, and create a platform for mutual experience exchange.

A community-oriented approach benefits every professional field by fostering networking and experience exchange, which enhances workers' expertise.

Kyiv IT Cluster partners with foreign experts and universities, supports higher education, and launched Kyiv Smart City to digitize civic services for transparency. UNIT.City, an innovation park, hosts 108 resident companies and keeps expanding.

For example, Kyiv IT Cluster regularly collaborates with foreign specialists and Kyiv universities, establishing valuable links abroad and supporting higher education. This Cluster also initiated the Kyiv Smart City project: a bid to digitize the civic processes of the entire city, making everything more transparent and accessible.

The Kyiv IT sector is also greatly supported by UNIT.City, an innovation park and infrastructure for business development.

Similar work is underway in other major Ukrainian IT Clusters in Lviv, Kharkiv, and Dnipro. Their legacy includes Lviv IT Arena, Lviv IT Jazz conference, KidIT and StudentIT platforms, and other conferences and social initiatives.

Nowadays, the Ukrainian IT market is competitive, competent, and ever-growing. There are over 4,000 well-established companies, with new ones emerging constantly.

chapters.title: Competing on the world stage
chapters.text:
Technology now drives a growing share of the global economy each year. Ukraine's sector expands faster than the worldwide average — about twenty percent annually versus roughly eleven to twenty-six percent over five years from 2014 to 2019 — yet substantial room remains for deeper domestic demand.

Outsourcing remains a strategic strength. Ukrainian engineers earn respect internationally, and productivity benchmarks have been cited at seven times that of Indian competitors in some analyses. Ukraine ranks second globally in IT outsourcing and third on Upwork for advanced technical talent.

While studying successful startups in your field, explore international examples and draw inspiration from various approaches.

Homegrown products also gain traction. Petcube, founded in Kyiv, builds pet cameras and software; The Europas named it Europe's best hardware startup in 2014. Grammarly, the AI writing assistant used by more than twenty million people daily, began in Ukraine as well.

Depositphotos operates one of the largest royalty-free libraries with 125 million assets. Allset tackles restaurant pre-ordering across more than 2,500 US locations — proof that Ukrainian teams can win far from home.

Grammarly is a nifty AI-based typing assistant that reviews spelling, grammar, punctuation, and stylistic aspects to better your writing and online communication. The product is immensely popular and used by over 20 million people daily.

No less impressive are Depositphotos and Allset. Depositphotos is a royalty-free content platform and one of the largest photo libraries. Allset focuses on food delivery and pre-ordering available in more than 2,500 restaurants in large US cities.

Did you know? The Headway writing team uses Ukrainian-founded Grammarly to make the summaries you read the best they can be!

chapters.title: Education shapes each generation
chapters.text:
Universities mold the values and methods each IT cohort carries into industry. Ukraine graduates roughly sixteen thousand technology students annually, and related majors rank among the most popular nationwide. Yet classroom content often lags market needs.

The main issue that Ukrainian IT students face upon graduation is the considerable gap between what they learned in their classes and the reality of the industry. Outdated curriculums leave holes that demand months of supplemental training detached from real hiring expectations.

But this doesn't mean IT education in Ukraine isn't worth it. On the contrary, the general level of graduates' basic technical knowledge and skills is consistently high, which makes it easier for them to fill in the gaps in their training upon graduation. On average, Ukrainian IT graduates begin working on real projects after only three to six months of additional training.

A combination of formal education and practical on-field training will help you stand out in the job market.

Institutions that partner closely with employers update fastest. Leading choices include Taras Shevchenko National University of Kyiv (KNU), Kyiv Polytechnic Institute (KPI), Kyiv-Mohyla Academy (KMA), Lviv Polytechnic, Kharkiv University, Dnipro University, and Donetsk University.

KNU, founded in 1834, housed Victor Glushkov's Institute of Cybernetics — a world-class research center. KMA alumni frequently join Google, Amazon, IBM, and Microsoft.

For example, KNU, founded in 1834, is where Victor Glushkov and colleagues formed the Institute of Cybernetics — one of the world's leading scientific centers. Graduates of KMA are sought-after professionals that work at Google, Amazon, IBM, Microsoft, and other titans of the industry.

Though it has flaws, IT education in Ukraine is solid and ever-developing. Universities are putting a lot of work into staying relevant and competitive, and the emergence of various exchange programs significantly boosts this process. The future of Ukrainian IT students looks bright!

chapters.title: Economic and civic impact
chapters.text:
IT delivers measurable economic leverage. Each technology job reportedly supports about thirty positions in energy, construction, trade, and adjacent services. Digitization also modernizes traditional industries that once resisted change.

Influence extends beyond revenue. Since independence, Ukraine weathered Maidan protests and ongoing war with Russia. The tech community responded repeatedly.

The most well-known and influential events are the Three Maidans (protests and demonstrations on the Kyiv Maidan Square) and the Russian-Ukrainian war.

Throughout these tumultuous events, the Ukrainian IT industry has stood by its people, helping in many subtle ways. During the Revolution of Dignity in 2014, which united close to four million Ukrainians in a fight against the corrupt and anti-Ukrainian government, the IT community set up the "IT#Namet" (IT#Tent), which began as a WiFi hotspot and consultation service for peaceful protesters. It evolved into a coordination hub distributing medicine and supplies to people injured by police violence.

As the Revolution progressed, the IT#Namet became a coordination center for collecting resources for the people, including medicines for the victims of police brutality.

The industry didn't fail to step up when the Russian-Ukrainian war began either. Many IT specialists worked toward strengthening the army's defense capacity and developing means of individual protection.

There is no revolutionary way out of a bad economic predicament. The past keeps pulling you back. There is only one way out — to rise to the challenge and make a bold move. ~ Andrei Kirilenko, PhD

These episodes show Ukrainian IT as both highly skilled and socially responsible — professionals who improve daily life through products like Kyiv Smart City, where residents pay bills, vote on initiatives, enroll children in kindergarten, register pets, and browse events from one mobile app.

Thanks to the robust IT front, Ukraine is more digitized than ever: smart cities and e-government are nothing new to Ukrainians, who can do virtually anything over their phones.

Digitalization drives social change and development, positively impacting various sectors.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Information technology ranks among Ukraine's most valued assets — a pathway to prosperity and modernization recognized by government and citizens alike. Persistent challenges have not halted growth across multiple domains, and the digital sector continues climbing.

The future of the Ukrainian IT industry is currently in the hands of qualified and innovative young professionals that are more than ready to tackle new challenges and generate exciting ideas. They are free of Soviet constraints and in sync with the global market, which makes the potential of Ukrainian IT practically limitless. Wartime urgency may accelerate cybersecurity expertise; Ukraine could someday become a leader in digital defense.

The community remains collaborative and socially engaged, with ecosystems that nurture entrepreneurship across healthcare, education, finance, and e-commerce. Obstacles remain, yet awesome digital Ukraine keeps advancing.

The Ukrainian IT community is strong, socially responsible, and has the potential for implementing change on both local and global scales. The sector is ever-growing and always needs more people to move progress along. Moreover, Ukrainian IT fosters a vibrant ecosystem that nurtures creativity, entrepreneurship, and collaboration, driving progress across diverse domains, including healthcare, education, finance, and e-commerce.

Try this

The Awesome Ukraine series of books by the Osnovy publishing house covers various aspects of Ukrainian culture and contains wonderful photos and illustrations, so if you liked this summary, check out the original source.

Read it till the end?
